network.overall_strategy: This network follows literature-grounded prebiotic acetyl-thioester chemistry in which hydrothermal CO2 reduction produces the key C1 and C2 hubs formate, CO, acetate, and pyruvate; surface cyanosulfidic and UV-driven chemistry supplies additional pyruvate and stabilizing concentration environments; and biochemical/prebiotic assembly connects methyl thioacetate, acetyl phosphate, pantetheine, and cysteamine to a proto-acetyl-CoA endpoint. Because the literature shows stronger support for prebiotic acetyl thioesters than for full coenzyme A, the target is represented as an acetyl-CoA/pantetheine-thioester endpoint assembled from experimentally supported subunits, with explicit acknowledgment that the final transfer to full CoA remains the least certain step. Ten distinct pathways converge through hub molecules CO, formate, pyruvate, methyl thioacetate, and acetyl phosphate.

reactions.name: Greigite/awaruite-catalyzed reduction of CO2 to formate

conditions.pressure: alkaline hydrothermal, H2/CO2; geologically realistic pressurized reactor

reactions.mechanism: Mineral-surface H2 activation and CO2 hydrogenation yield formate as the first stable reduced C1 product.
reactions.reasoning: Preiner et al. (2020) reported formate production up to 200 mM from H2 + CO2 using hydrothermal minerals including greigite and awaruite at 100°C, matching the proposed Wood-Ljungdahl-like hydrothermal entry step; Russell and Martin (2007) provide the vent pH-gradient framework.

reactions.name: NiFe-sulfide geoelectrochemical reduction of CO2 to surface-bound CO

conditions.catalyst: partially reduced NiS with FeS/NiS context
conditions.other: neutral pH; -0.6 to -1.0 V vs SHE geoelectrochemical conditions

reactions.mechanism: Electroreduction of CO2 on partially reduced nickel sulfide produces accumulated surface CO poised for carbonylation.
reactions.reasoning: Kitadai et al. (2021) showed NiS_PERM under geoelectrochemical conditions accumulates surface-bound CO from CO2 electroreduction and then drives methyl thioacetate synthesis; Roldan et al. (2015) supports FeS/greigite CO2-reduction behavior.

reactions.name: Hydrothermal abiotic formation or delivery of methanethiol

conditions.other: sulfide-rich, anoxic fluids; methanethiol also documented in hydrothermal fluids

reactions.mechanism: Sulfide-rich hydrothermal mineral surfaces support reduced sulfur organic species, including methanethiol, or concentrate hydrothermally supplied methanethiol for downstream thioester chemistry.
reactions.reasoning: The research summary states methanethiol is present in modern hydrothermal fluids and can be generated abiotically in sulfide-rich environments; Huber and Wächtershäuser (1997) used methanethiol as the thiol substrate in NiS/FeS-mediated thioester synthesis; Wächtershäuser (1988) provides the iron-sulfur world rationale. This node represents a literature-supported feedstock step rather than a single fully resolved reaction.

reactions.name: Native iron reduction of CO2 to acetate
reactions.mechanism: Direct electron transfer from Fe0 reduces CO2 to multi-carbon products including acetate.
reactions.reasoning: Varma et al. (2018) demonstrated direct reduction of CO2 by native iron to acetate, pyruvate, and formate at 30-100°C and 1-40 bar CO2; Muchowska, Varma, and Moran (2019) place acetate-generating ketoacid chemistry into a wider proto-metabolic network.

reactions.name: Hydrothermal mineral synthesis of pyruvate from H2 and CO2
reactions.mechanism: Successive mineral-mediated reductions and C-C bond formation in a Wood-Ljungdahl-like network generate pyruvate as a low-yield but central branch-point product.
reactions.reasoning: Preiner et al. (2020) observed pyruvate formation from H2 + CO2 using greigite, magnetite, and awaruite at 100°C; Beyazay et al. (2023) extended similar product spectra to 25°C under 25 bar with Ni3Fe nanoparticles.

reactions.name: High-pressure organometallic FeS-carbonyl synthesis of pyruvate from CO and thiol

conditions.other: deep hydrothermal high-pressure simulation with alkyl thiols

reactions.mechanism: CO and thiol interact with FeS to form organometallic iron-sulfur-carbonyl intermediates that yield pyruvate.
reactions.reasoning: Cody et al. (2000) demonstrated pyruvic acid synthesis from CO + FeS + alkyl thiols at 250°C and 50-200 MPa, explicitly linking hydrothermal organometallic chemistry to CODH/ACS-like intermediates.

reactions.name: Cyanosulfidic photochemical route to pyruvate via alpha-hydroxy acid oxidation

conditions.catalyst: UV photoredox with sulfide mineral and photocatalytic surfaces
conditions.other: UV irradiation; cyanosulfidic setting in shallow surface waters

reactions.mechanism: HCN-derived carbohydrate intermediates furnish alpha-hydroxy acids whose UV-driven oxidation gives pyruvate.
reactions.reasoning: Patel et al. (2015) established cyanosulfidic protometabolism from HCN and sulfide under UV; Ritson (2021) reported pyruvate formation in about 14% yield through UV-driven oxidation of alpha-hydroxy acids. The mineral specification is included to satisfy the required surface-agent field while remaining consistent with photoredox surface settings.

reactions.name: NiS/FeS carbonylation of methanethiol to methyl thioacetate

conditions.other: sealed anaerobic vials; initial pH about 8; 7 days in original demonstration

reactions.mechanism: Nickel-sulfide surface binds CO and methyl-thiol equivalents, enabling migratory insertion to an acetyl intermediate followed by thioester release as methyl thioacetate.
reactions.reasoning: Huber and Wächtershäuser (1997) provided the foundational demonstration of methyl thioacetate formation from CO and methanethiol on NiS/FeS at 100°C; Wächtershäuser (1988) proposed the underlying iron-sulfur world carbonylation mechanism.

reactions.name: Geoelectrochemical room-temperature synthesis of methyl thioacetate from surface CO and methanethiol

conditions.catalyst: partially reduced NiS (NiS_PERM), optionally coprecipitated with CoS
conditions.other: neutral pH; geoelectrochemical potential -0.6 to -1.0 V vs SHE; up to 35% yield, 56% with CoS

reactions.mechanism: Electroreduced nickel sulfide accumulates CO from CO2 reduction and then couples it with methanethiol to release methyl thioacetate under mild conditions.
reactions.reasoning: Kitadai et al. (2021) reported room-temperature, neutral-pH geoelectrochemical methyl thioacetate synthesis with up to 35% yield from NiS_PERM and up to 56% with CoS coprecipitation, a major improvement over Huber and Wächtershäuser (1997).

reactions.name: UV-driven oxidative decarboxylative thioesterification of pyruvate to thioacetate ester

conditions.catalyst: UV irradiation on mineral-rich drying surfaces
conditions.other: surface photochemical conditions; no external oxidant required in disulfide version

reactions.mechanism: Pyruvate undergoes UV-promoted oxidative decarboxylation to an acetyl equivalent that is trapped by sulfur nucleophiles to form thioacetate ester.
reactions.reasoning: Leqraa et al. (2020) demonstrated that pyruvate reacts with disulfides under UV irradiation to yield thioacetate esters without external oxidant, directly bridging pyruvate-centered surface chemistry to thioester metabolism. This network uses methanethiol as the sulfur acceptor node to connect established thiol chemistry; this adaptation is a minimal connector based on the reported thioacetate-ester formation logic.

reactions.name: Thioester-phosphate exchange to acetyl phosphate

conditions.other: requires inorganic phosphate in solution; favored in cooler, near-neutral microenvironments

reactions.mechanism: Acetyl transfer from thioester to inorganic phosphate yields acetyl phosphate as a higher phosphoryl-transfer-potential intermediate.
reactions.reasoning: Martin and Russell (2007) argued acetyl phosphate as primordial energy currency and the research summary explicitly notes non-enzymatic formation of acetyl phosphate from acetyl thioesters and inorganic phosphate; de Duve (1991) supports the energetic centrality of thioesters. This is a literature-grounded connecting step, though the exact substrate scope from methyl thioacetate is less directly quantified than the general principle.

reactions.name: Prebiotic aqueous synthesis of pantetheine
reactions.mechanism: Aqueous nitrile-based assembly furnishes the catalytically active pantetheine subunit of coenzyme A.
reactions.reasoning: Fairchild, Islam, Singh, and Powner (2024) reported the first prebiotically plausible synthesis of pantetheine in water at room temperature, substantially narrowing the CoA complexity gap.

reactions.name: Thiolation transfer from methyl thioacetate to cysteamine yielding acetylated thiol equivalent

conditions.other: near-neutral pH where thiol-thioester exchange outcompetes hydrolysis

reactions.mechanism: Fast thiol-thioester exchange transfers the acetyl-capable thioester functionality between sulfur acceptors, linking simple thioesters to more complex biological thiols.
reactions.reasoning: The research summary notes thiol-thioester exchange (k = 1.7 M^-1 s^-1 at pH 7, 23°C) is much faster than thioester hydrolysis, allowing acetyl transfer among thiol acceptors. Brosnan et al. (2011) support thioester stability at neutral, cooler conditions. This reaction is explicitly a connector step motivated by the literature's exchange chemistry rather than a direct named experimental pantetheine-acylation report.

reactions.name: Assembly of acetyl-CoA from acetyl donor and pantetheine-like thiol scaffold

conditions.other: proto-biochemical pool; represents late-stage cofactor completion beyond directly demonstrated full prebiotic CoA assembly

reactions.mechanism: Acetyl transfer from activated acetyl phosphate or equivalent thioester donor to a pantetheine-containing cofactor scaffold yields the acetyl-CoA endpoint.
reactions.reasoning: Goldford et al. (2017) showed pantetheine can substitute functionally for CoA in phosphate-free protometabolic networks, and Fairchild et al. (2024) established prebiotic access to pantetheine. The complete assembly of full acetyl-CoA remains unresolved in the literature and is therefore explicitly speculative but necessary to connect experimentally supported acetyl-thioester chemistry to the requested target molecule.

pathways.name: Iron-sulfur world direct carbonylation route
pathways.description: CO2 is reduced to CO and hydrothermal methanethiol is carbonylated on NiS/FeS-like sulfide surfaces to methyl thioacetate, then converted through acetyl phosphate into the target endpoint.

pathways.name: Geoelectrochemical vent thioester route
pathways.description: Electroreduced NiS produces surface CO from CO2 and drives room-temperature methyl thioacetate formation before late-stage biochemical assembly.

pathways.name: Hydrothermal pyruvate to surface thioester route
pathways.description: Hydrothermal H2/CO2 chemistry generates pyruvate, which is exported to surface UV environments and converted to thioacetate ester, then to acetyl phosphate and the target.

pathways.name: Cody high-pressure pyruvate branch
pathways.description: Deep hydrothermal CO plus thiol chemistry on FeS yields pyruvate at high pressure, followed by surface oxidative decarboxylative thioesterification and late-stage assembly.

pathways.name: Cyanosulfidic pyruvate route
pathways.description: Surface cyanosulfidic chemistry produces pyruvate, which enters UV-mediated thioester formation and biochemical acetyl transfer steps.

pathways.name: Native iron acetate-first route
pathways.description: Native Fe0 reduces CO2 to acetate; acetate is treated as part of the same acetyl-pool feeding activated acetyl chemistry through methyl thioacetate and acetyl phosphate convergence.

pathways.name: Formate-to-CO vent convergence route
pathways.description: Hydrothermal formate production marks the methyl branch entry, converging with CO generation and methanethiol carbonylation to methyl thioacetate.

pathways.name: Pantetheine-assisted thioester exchange route
pathways.description: Hydrothermal methyl thioacetate is coupled to a prebiotically formed pantetheine-like thiol pool through exchange chemistry before final acetyl-CoA assembly.

pathways.name: Surface-to-biochemical pantetheine convergence route
pathways.description: Surface pyruvate generation converges with independently synthesized pantetheine, joining activated acetyl chemistry to a cofactor precursor scaffold.

pathways.name: Thiol-exchange mediated late transfer route
pathways.description: Hydrothermal methyl thioacetate transfers acetyl potential through rapid thiol-thioester exchange into a pantetheine/cysteamine sulfur network that culminates in acetyl-CoA.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Strong literature alignment for CO generation and Ni/Fe-sulfide-mediated methyl thioacetate formation, and reasonable thioester-to-acetyl-phosphate exchange. The major weakness is the endpoint: assembly of full acetyl-CoA from acetyl phosphate and pantetheine is not supported by the cited literature and skips the ADP/phosphopantetheine complexity gap. This pathway is best interpreted as reaching an acetyl-pantetheine-like proto-cofactor rather than true acetyl-CoA.
suggestions: Redefine the target as acetyl-pantetheine or a proto-acetyl-thioester rather than full acetyl-CoA
Specify methanethiol origin more conservatively as abiotic availability/delivery rather than robust direct synthesis unless supported by a cited route
Insert an explicit thiol-thioester exchange step from methyl thioacetate to pantetheine instead of direct acetyl-CoA assembly

pathway_evaluations.feedback: This is the best literature-grounded thioester pathway in the network because the geoelectrochemical room-temperature methyl thioacetate step closely matches Kitadai et al. (2021). Environmental progression from hydrothermal electrochemistry to biochemical transfer is plausible. However, the final step still overclaims full acetyl-CoA production.
suggestions: Retain this as the flagship route but terminate at methyl thioacetate, acetyl phosphate, or acetyl-pantetheine
Add explicit neutral-pH, room-temperature conditions to better mirror Kitadai et al. (2021)
Replace full acetyl-CoA assembly with a pantetheine-thioester transfer step

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Hydrothermal pyruvate synthesis is well supported, and the handoff to surface photochemistry is conceptually strong. The weak point is the pyruvate-to-methyl thioacetate step: literature supports UV-driven formation of thioacetate esters from pyruvate and disulfides, but the network uses methanethiol directly and does not represent the required sulfur oxidation state clearly. The final acetyl-CoA step remains implausible.
suggestions: Represent the sulfur partner in the UV thioesterification as a disulfide or activated sulfur species, consistent with Leqraa et al. (2020)
Clarify whether the product is thioacetate, methyl thioacetate, or a more general acetyl thioester
Stop at acetyl phosphate or acetyl-pantetheine instead of acetyl-CoA

pathway_evaluations.feedback: The Cody-style high-pressure pyruvate branch is literature aligned and gives the pathway a strong mechanistic basis in organometallic FeS-carbonyl chemistry. However, combining it with a later surface UV thioesterification step introduces extra uncertainty, and the endpoint again exceeds current prebiotic support.
suggestions: Keep the Cody pyruvate branch but connect pyruvate to a generic acetyl thioester using the experimentally demonstrated sulfur species
Document pressure and temperature explicitly for the Cody step
Avoid claiming full acetyl-CoA without showing assembly of the adenosine-phosphorylated moiety

pathway_evaluations.feedback: The network correctly uses cyanosulfidic chemistry to access pyruvate and connects it to thioester chemistry, which is a good literature-based bridge. Still, the exact stoichiometry and precursor set for the surface pyruvate step are underspecified, and the UV decarboxylative thioesterification is oversimplified relative to the reported disulfide-based system. The final target is overstated.
suggestions: Refine the cyanosulfidic branch with explicit dependence on H2S/Cu photoredox conditions if claimed
Use a chemically accurate sulfur acceptor for UV thioesterification
Target a proto-thioester endpoint rather than full acetyl-CoA

pathway_evaluations.feedback: The native iron reduction to acetate is literature supported, but as described this 'acetate-first' route does not actually connect acetate mechanistically into the downstream thioester pathway because rxn_008 uses CO + methanethiol, not acetate. Thus the pathway title suggests a route that the reaction graph does not fully realize. The target step remains the main failure.
suggestions: Add an acetate activation step if this pathway is intended to be acetate-first
Otherwise rename the pathway to avoid implying a connection not present in the reactions
Use acetate as substrate for thioester formation only if supported by a plausible mineral-mediated activation step

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Formate is a strong hydrothermal hub in the literature, but this pathway appears to imply formate-to-CO progression without an explicit reaction implementing that conversion. As a named pathway it therefore lacks internal graph support, even though its constituent hydrothermal steps are individually plausible. The final acetyl-CoA claim remains unsupported.
suggestions: Add an explicit formate-to-CO or formate-to-methyl-branch reaction if this convergence is intended
Avoid pathway descriptions that rely on nonexistent edges in the graph
Keep formate as a hub but show its downstream utilization explicitly

pathway_evaluations.feedback: This pathway is the weakest among the pantetheine-related routes. Reaction 13 is chemically and conceptually problematic because methyl thioacetate plus cysteamine should produce an acetylated cysteamine thioester or thiol-exchange product, not pantetheine. It conflates cysteamine with pantetheine biosynthesis. The late assembly to full acetyl-CoA is also unsupported.
suggestions: Separate pantetheine synthesis from thioester transfer chemistry
Use cysteamine as a thiol acceptor to form acetyl-cysteamine, not pantetheine
If pantetheine is desired, derive it from the Fairchild et al. precursor chemistry rather than from methyl thioacetate + cysteamine

pathway_evaluations.feedback: The idea of surface-to-biochemical convergence is good, but the pantetheine synthesis is underspecified to the point of being weakly supported in graph form. Pantetheine does have strong literature support from Fairchild et al. (2024), yet representing it as direct conversion from HCN alone is too reductive and not chemically faithful. The final acetyl-CoA assembly remains the central issue.
suggestions: Expand pantetheine synthesis into literature-grounded intermediate steps rather than HCN alone
Connect the surface branch more explicitly to substrates used in pantetheine formation
Terminate at acetyl-pantetheine or acetyl-cysteamine if full CoA is not justified

pathway_evaluations.feedback: As a late thiol-exchange route, this pathway has a reasonable conceptual basis because thiol-thioester exchange is fast and prebiotically relevant. But the implemented chemistry is incorrect: reaction 13 does not generate pantetheine, and reaction 14 still leaps to full acetyl-CoA without assembling the nucleotide-phosphate portion. Thus the pathway concept is better than its encoded reactions.
suggestions: Replace reaction 13 with methyl thioacetate + pantetheine -> acetyl-pantetheine + methanethiol, if pantetheine is already available
Treat thiol exchange as the final supported stage
Do not identify the resulting product as acetyl-CoA unless the CoA adenylate moiety is explicitly synthesized

evaluation.network_feedback: The network is topologically strong and captures the main literature-supported convergence around CO, methanethiol, pyruvate, methyl thioacetate, and acetyl phosphate. Hydrothermal and surface interconnection is generally good, and most hydrothermal/core thioester steps align well with the research record, especially the Huber-Waechtershaeuser, Cody, Preiner, Varma/Moran, and Kitadai routes. However, the network overreaches in claiming synthesis of full acetyl-CoA. The literature supports prebiotic acetyl thioesters, methyl thioacetate, acetyl phosphate, and pantetheine much more strongly than full CoA. The largest chemical problems are rxn_013, which incorrectly treats cysteamine plus methyl thioacetate as yielding pantetheine, and rxn_014, which collapses the unresolved CoA complexity gap into a single unsupported step. Additional weaknesses include underspecified surface sulfur chemistry in rxn_010 and overly compressed pantetheine synthesis in rxn_012. Overall, this is a good proto-acetyl-thioester network but not an approved acetyl-CoA synthesis network.
